title: Pain aux raisins
subtitle: Laminated Danish pastries rolled with crème pâtissière and sultanas
excerpt: A laminated Danish pastry dough spread with vanilla crème pâtissière and plump sultanas, rolled into a log, sliced into rounds, and baked until deeply golden. The layers shatter at the edge and soften toward the centre. Makes twelve.
sourceNotes: Pain aux raisins is a French and Danish laminated pastry documented in European patisserie from the nineteenth century. The form belongs to the Vienna pastry tradition (Viennoiserie) introduced to France in the 1830s. Urbain Dubois, La Cuisine Classique (1856), public domain: laminated yeasted dough filled with crème pâtissière and dried fruit is documented in the Viennoiserie chapter. The specific combination of vanilla crème pâtissière and sultanas in a laminated spiral is consistent with the codified French patisserie tradition recorded in pre-1928 professional guides.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze unbaked sliced rounds on a tray, then bag once solid. Prove from frozen overnight in the fridge, then bake as normal.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: After the final lamination fold, wrap the dough and refrigerate overnight. Roll, fill, and slice the next day.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven; the pastries are done when deeply golden on top and the layers are visible and set at the edges; 20–22 minutes

glossaryTerms.definition: The process of laminating a yeasted dough with a block of cold butter to create alternating layers. A cold butter block (beurrage) is enveloped in the dough, then the combined piece is rolled and folded in a series of letter-folds (turns). Each fold multiplies the layers: after three folds the dough contains 27 distinct butter layers. Chilling the dough between folds keeps the butter cold and solid; if the butter melts into the dough, the layers are lost and the result is a brioche-like crumb rather than a flaky one.

content.text: The lamination requires three separate folds with 60 to 80 minutes of chilling between each. Plan 4 to 5 hours from mixing to baking; the chilling cannot be rushed.
is the only technically demanding stage; keep the work surface and rolling pin cold, and work quickly.

items.prepNote: cold, bashed flat between two sheets of paper to a 20 cm square
soaked in cold water for 30 minutes, drained

content.text: Combine the {{strong-bread-flour}}, {{yeast-fast-action}}, {{caster-sugar}} (dough portion), and {{sea-salt-fine}} in a bowl. Add the warm {{whole-milk}} (dough portion), {{eggs}} (1 egg), and {{unsalted-butter}} (softened, dough portion). Mix to a smooth dough, knead briefly for 2 minutes, shape into a flat rectangle, wrap, and refrigerate for 30 minutes.

Encase the cold {{unsalted-butter}} block (lamination portion) inside the dough: roll the dough to double the size of the butter, lay the butter in the centre, fold the dough over it, and pinch the edges to seal. Roll the parcel out to a long rectangle (about 20 × 50 cm). Fold the bottom third up and the top third down (a letter fold), wrap, and refrigerate for 60 to 80 minutes. Repeat twice more for a total of 3 folds, chilling between each. After the final fold, refrigerate for at least 60 minutes.

Heat the {{whole-milk}} (crème portion) in a saucepan until steaming. Whisk the {{eggs}} (2 eggs), {{caster-sugar}} (crème portion), and {{cornflour}} together in a bowl. Pour the hot milk over the egg mixture in a thin stream, whisking constantly. Return to the pan and stir over medium heat until the custard thickens and bubbles; about 3 minutes. Remove from the heat, stir in the {{vanilla-extract}}, press clingfilm directly onto the surface, and refrigerate until cold.

Roll the laminated dough out to a rectangle of about 30 × 40 cm. Spread a thin, even layer of the cold crème pâtissière over the surface, leaving a 2 cm border at the far long edge. Scatter the drained {{sultanas}} over the cream. Roll the dough tightly from the near long edge toward the far edge. Slice into 12 rounds about 3 cm thick and arrange cut-side up on lined baking trays, spaced well apart. Cover and prove at room temperature for 60 minutes until puffed. Preheat the oven to 200°C fan. Brush gently with the beaten {{eggs}} glaze and bake for 20 to 22 minutes until deeply golden. Cool on a rack.
